transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hello.
Hi there.
How's it going?
Nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you.
I'm Andy.
I lead customer operations for GrowthX.
And we have Matthew joining, who is our chief content officer.
Nice to meet you both.
Likewise.
Hello, folks.
How you doing?
So, I mean, we can jump right in.
Want to make the most of the time.
Really appreciate you guys scheduling something same day.
I know that can be hard.
But I think we would love to get a sense of kind of what you've experienced in the transition, where you feel like things are missing.
I think a lot about

It's processing people, and so transitioning out of Strategy Sprint, and putting clients with the right teams, and Panzar is, as his title says, Chief Content Officer, so he is thinking about our product pipelines, outputs, so I think both of those things sound like they're not quite matching right now, so we want to just get a sense of what's happening and how we can fix it.

Yeah, so, I'll try to summarize it as much as possible, after the first, and I forgot all the names, because a few people, and sometimes it's gone by, but we had an initial stint, I forgot his name, to be honest with you, very, very early on, to kick off things.
He then introduced us to our, you know, I guess, our first team, which was Sydney.
And with them, I think there was a discussion on, okay, well, let's build a first layer of strategy, or at least a first hypothesis around the strategy that we wanted to pursue.

Define an area that we wanted to develop content for, start developing the first few assets, calibrate the model, see how things are working, and incrementally add more types of blogs to the pipeline so that we can see what the outcome is and how quickly we can both generate as well as review and publish content.
So the first phase was more like exploration and discussion and notion capture, you know, some of the...
We obviously invested a lot of time, I would say, in the first month in particular, to train them up on what we do, on our audience, and the type of content that we write, our product.
That we are a B2B organization, selling to physical security, which is run by operators with significant hands-on experience on things.
But we're also an AI provider, so there's a whole aspect around the technology itself.
That is, I would say, relatively sophisticated.
The market is noisy, there's a lot of...
Claims out there of people doing stuff, which means terminology may be confusing.
And there's a relatively broad set of use cases that could span around functional use cases like threat detection, access control, monitoring, forensic, but also vertical.
And so each vertical slash building type that you're securing has a different set of priorities, issues, like school is different than an office.
So we invested some time to bring the two up to speed, particularly Sydney, who was our primary really contact point in terms of interacting on actual content.
And And And And And And
And she helped us a lot also on evolving the models so that we would get good results.
Now, we were just about to hit the more difficult things, I would say, things like competitive blogs or more verticalized or expanding beyond the first vertical.
We picked schools as the first vertical, and we were starting to go into, okay, let's see how the model does on hospital.
And so we were hitting on some initial limitations, but we produced some amount of content.

We worked with her quite a bit to kind of get her up to speed so that she would understand the nuances of what we're doing, what's the difference between a threat versus an event versus, you know, when we say we have, we reduce alarms by 95%.
What does that mean and what?

Context, you know, so that you just don't sound like a marketing machine that just spits out, you know, random taglines at the appropriate time and also kind of sounding a little bit like tools ALC, right?
I mean, there's also a cultural aspect here, how we interpret our content, to be honest, and many different organizations have different requirements, but we...
We put a high degree of importance on the quality, how the blogs read, how the...
Are we delivering some value?
Does it sound authentic to a technical audience or to an operator?
Are we just regurgitating stuff?
Or, you know, is it like...
How...
How...
How easy it is to...
Determine whether this is human-generated versus AI-generated.
Right.
We wanted to sound like a human, understanding that 80% should come out of the model so that we can work faster.
So we put quite a bit of emphasis on that aspect, and we were starting to get into a group with her in terms of her being able to incorporate feedback, but also understanding our business.
And then all of a sudden, that gets unplugged, right, because of the process that you guys have.
Frankly, it came as a surprise.
I didn't understand it initially that there was going to be this switch.
I probably missed something.
But everyone on the team here, and Maurice, can obviously testify, we all got concerned about the transition.
Not because whoever transitions in.
Intuit won't be able to eventually get to it.
I mean, unless you're really staffing, underperforming people, which, you know, it's not the starting point.
But, you know, it didn't take us a week to get Sydney up to speed.
And we're a small company.
We don't have time to repeat the same cycle with someone new.
And I'll say Sydney is also pretty good.
So I think we were pretty happy with how we were interacting with her, communication, fit our style.
We, you know, in terms of content creation and in terms of logic and discussions around this is where we need to go and how it works, I think it was starting to work really well.
And we were also kind of like a little disappointed from that angle, right?
Because, you know, you got...
got...
You
Familiar with someone, you're starting to work well together, changing the team is not, it's not ideal, right?
So, both Aida and Simi said, oh, we're going to, you know, support the new team ramping up, but I think that overlap was really minimal.
And, I think, I mean, in the last call, they weren't even on anymore, so I'm assuming that at this point, they considered their job done, but I can tell you that the new team is not ready.
Okay, so that's step one, and I hate to have to redo the same thing, even if they have recorded material that they can go read or listen to, it will take time.
And I'm seeing some of the same mistakes that were happening at the beginning of the cycle.
So Sydney is being repeated.
Why?
Because you just don't know.
You just don't know the environment.
You don't know the audience.
And a document will never tell you enough until you're in it for a while.
Even Sydney had, like, I remember the whole alarms.
Like, one of the things that we pitch is that we reduce alarms.
We reduce alarms by false positives by 95%.
And it took a few cycles for her to understand when.
And initially, we were just, like, regurgitating this as a general statement that we could make across any use case.
But it's not the case.
It's a specific use case in which we reduce this.
So when we put it in a blog post or in the context of something, it belongs in a certain domain.
You just don't spread it randomly.
And there's other examples like this.
And so I'm seeing that we basically have to...
Reset, and it's a big waste of time for us, because we have many other things to do, and even dedicating more cycles to this is not ideal.
I think the last thing I would say is, the new team, the two observations, in terms of communications, one, and both their names begin with M, and I don't want to confuse them.
Mariana and Mara.
Mariana and Mara.
So Mara has been a little passive so far.
She's been on the call, but didn't really participate much, in terms of setting the pace.
Mariana is definitely more active, and taking the lead on stuff, but also has been a little bit more chaotic.
In um...

In the way we have been discussing content prioritization, so we had a plan, or not a plan, but we had a framework that we were using with Sydney, now all of a sudden, different way of looking at the problems are being introduced, and that confused us a lot over the last couple of calls, like, okay, well, are we optimizing for keywords, or are we optimizing for a campaign?
If we're doing a campaign on a particular use case, then what are the keywords within that, and what is the general structure that we want to take?

Because one of the things that I think we're still going through, also with Sydney, to some extent, was organizing the content around a macro objective, that we can repeat to the, we can explain to the company, not macro objective, but around a theme, use case, slash vertical.

Objectives are on SEO and whatnot, but a little bit more thematic in its description and encapsulation than just like, there's a gazillion keywords that we need to capture and here's like a bootload of content that we put out because that's more difficult for people outside of marketing to follow.

So our objective was to go back to sales team and others in the company and say, hey, this month or two months or whatever, this period of time, we're going to dedicate it to develop this campaign around gun detections for school.
Then next one would be, we're going to do unauthorized access for corporate security, right?
And a whole bunch of assets mapped to the clusters would be generated.
And that way, we can bring the company along a little bit.
Versus a different approach.
So that's where we were trying to go towards, but we never really got to do it because the transition kind of put the whole new challenge in front of us.
And I don't think that the teams were, when you were in thinking, in alignment to that principle.
Whereas that's not what we've witnessed.
So that's where we are at.
I mean, nothing bad has happened.
It's just, I don't think we should have gone through this transition.
feels unnecessary work on our end.
Slow down.
Yeah, I would agree.
It sounds like you've hit some, as you said, slowdowns.
And certainly shouldn't have to put in the effort twice.
Whereas, I...

I heard a couple of places where we can bring in the context that you have already shared, and that can feed not just in terms of Mariana and Mara learning the kind of the things that you guys have trained Sydney on and the way that you approach problems and how you think about structuring your content and the cadence of what's going out, but also feed it directly into the AI.
And I think Marcel mentioned on the call that we are rolling out more agentic pipelines.
That's not going to be the silver bullet here.
It sounds like a lot of this is about making sure that we're lining up with the strategy that you guys had agreed on and the pace and direction that you were thinking about.
over the next few months.
It's a little difficult, but so I
I didn't bring up the content itself because it's hard for me to fully understand where the AI begins and where the human writer starts from the outside.
I will say that I have experience with AirOps that I implemented in my previous role, and we built out probably an equivalent flow to what we're doing here.
So I have some experience in recognizing issues of using LLMs to create content.
And I'm recognizing some of the patterns of things that I was trying to resolve that I resolved in my past.
And we had these discussions with Mara and Mariana, and I gave them specific feedback as to how can we get to higher quality content.
you.
Thank Thank
To review, sorry, how can we get the first draft to be more on point versus having to spend a lot of time trying to understand what was the ultimate goal of the blog?
Because the review cycles were taking longer or they became too difficult.
But I don't think that that's, frankly, I wasn't putting that, putting it on them.
Unless you tell me that there's a heavy content of human editing, but I can't, I don't know from the outside how much they actually work on the blog after it comes out of the pipeline.
And so that I don't know.
But yeah, I mean, I can, I can give a little bit of context there if it's helpful.
And there's some things that I think we can improve that, that feed into your.
Concerns, right?
So I think the stuff you're articulating about, like the handoff and all of the work that you put in, is super valid.
the fact of the matter is, that is, I know you say nothing bad has happened, but something bad has happened or I wouldn't be talking to you.
The bad, I think, is like, you don't want to waste any more time, which is, you know, or you didn't waste the time initially, but wasting time would be doing it twice, right?
So we want to avoid that, if at all possible.
And I'm almost positive we can.

The agentic pipelines do, they're not a panacea, but they do address a lot of the issues that I'm sure you ran up against using Aerofs, where you need patterns that are, in patterns in your content that you want to appear, but in order to sell it to a human reader, those patterns need to be presented with enough variance, right?
They need a fuzzy logic framework around them.
That says, hey, I want these talking points in there, because that's what our company does.
remember Ambient, well, by the way, we wrote about them in 2022 on TechCrunch.
I remember writing about the funding.
And so I remember the whole pitch at the time.
And it doesn't seem like it's too much different now.
You know, folks are doing well.
No, you're probably right.
The, I think I remember, if I'm almost positive, I remember this, the jumping the fence example.
I remember that is coming across the editorial desk, the kids jumping the fence.

So like that, that scenario where you're trying to play out these kinds of very likely things that might occur on a campus or at a business or whatever, but you need them presented in humanist ways that don't feel alien or overly pattern generated to a human reader.
That is what the agentic pipelines are better at.
I don't, we don't need to go crazy on like the actual pipelines themselves.
Besides the fact that I think they will improve massively the.
The issues and foibles that you may have seen that crop up, especially because you're sensitive to it.
You've seen it firsthand working with like an Aerox style pipeline.

You've seen them crop up when LLMs are not given proper guidelines and there's not an agentic process where they have a validator and they can loop against that validator until they've adhered enough.
It's not enough, right?
Workflows are a sequence of events that just kind of like give you a zero or a one.
And the agentic processes are so much better.
But the big problem is really not, hey, these agentic pipelines can give you better content.
That's great.
They will, we will get that up and running for you.
I think you'll see a massive improvement.
It's more making sure that we take everything that you have said to Sydney, because it is recorded in one way or another.
And then make sure that it is properly parsed and context engineered into rules that the pipeline understands so that you do not ever have to say them again.
And then if we do have to say them once
Once in a while, it's just a reinforcement, and that's part of the natural editing process.
But you shouldn't have to give more than a handful of notes on any given batch of content, right?
That's the overall goal.
The pipelines right now, like the standardized pipelines we're doing, I would say you're probably looking at a human still operating at about 30 minutes to an hour per piece editing those pieces.

The agentic pipelines are down around five minutes of human editing, just to make sure that they make semantic adjustments, that they plus the content in a human way that, you know, you make a flourish of phrase or you go, oh, I remember the client wants this and you make a change.
That cuts down organically on the amount of feedback that you have to give, because we do not want you to have to redo what you just did with Sydney.
The goal for repeatability and consistency across whatever clusters or strategies that you do in the future is to make sure that your feedback is...
Institutionalized and context engineered properly for the pipelines.
Like, that's the big deal.
And that means that I don't want to say that, like, we always will need a talented editor to make sure that your pieces are up to snuff.
Because, you know, your content is important.
It's written by humans.
You have legal implications.
You have authoritative implications with your audience, especially because it's a technical audience or an audience looking for an implementation of this.
So all of those will always need a talented editor.
But it honestly shouldn't matter what editor it is.
You know, like, with the pipelines properly adhered, it should just be a talented editor can come in and help.
Because the pipeline will automatically tell them, hey, this is adhering to the technical requirements that are necessary here.
Yeah.
I see that all the editors are more on, like, stylistic stuff, frankly.
Unless we really want to bring them up to speed on.
stylistic stylistic stuff, stylistic stylistic stylistic
Understanding what we do.
And that takes time.
takes time for any one of us.
So if they need to play a role in actually delivering content that makes sense, then we definitely need to invest, make them part of the team.
It will require more than 30 minutes a week.
Granted, we're exchanging thoughts over Slack, so it's not just 30 minutes.
But you know what I mean.
It's like for any one of us here, you know, we leave it and breathe it, and we kind of absorb it over time.
And that's just what would be necessary for you guys, too.
It's not rocket science, but it's also not, you know, elementary school.
That's all.
So.
And Thank
Yeah, that's just time.
It's just time.

Now, I gave the team a bunch of suggestions for what we used to do with AeroOps when we implemented our model, which should help have those interim gates in terms of directing the model towards having a structure in the blog and what talking points go into that particular section and that type of stuff.
that should get to a final output that makes more, that you can actually relate to.
And it's not all over the place because sometimes, frankly, it feels as if the blog is written in sections.
You know, there's this section and it makes sense.
It makes, you know, sense within its own section.
But then there's thought forward.
Other sections that are not connected to it, or that feel completely tangential.
Yeah, it's part of the universal thing that we do, but why is it here?
Right, right, yeah.
So like that connecting tissue, maybe the human, inexperienced human editor that has spent enough time with the company would do basically what we are doing, right?
And could go in and just like delete all this section, or rewrite it this way, or connect the two sentences a little better, right?
But that would require some more hands-on, just day-to-day with us.
Yeah, that is actually, funny enough, you mentioned the segmentation problem.
It's like one of the big bits of impetus for us to move to the authentic pipeline was that the old pipeline was specifically generated because it had to work within roughly 800 to 1,000 token chunks.
It just was not possible for it.
To ingest enough context at a time for it to produce an entire article that adhered to, say, 2,000 tokens worth of detailed contextual instruction or advice.
You know, your writing guidelines, your company context, the subject of the article, all has to be stuffed, right, into it.
The new agentic pipelines are working with a much larger window, about 2,000 tokens per generation.
So what we end up doing is treating the entire article as a holistic output, as opposed to the older pipeline.
The older pipeline actually does generate every section separately, which is why it feels like, hey, this H2, this H2 feels really great.
And this makes a lot of logical sense.
And then the next one is like, wait a minute, we were just talking about this other thing.
Why are we not talking about that anymore?
And sometimes you can even see variances in stylistic nature, like, oh, this is really brief, and this is very laborious, or, you know, vice versa.
Oh, this has a great structure, and this one is a little mean.
Because they are given to an editor, there's basically run-in segments, and then it's given to a separate editor, the editor workflow, right?
And that workflow then assembles it.
It says, hey, you're an experienced editor, I've given you a bunch of segments, I need you to attach them to each other and make them adhere.
The new agentic pipeline does not generate stuff that way.
And our experience has been working with some very, very specific clients.
Like, have a client with four medical reviewers.
They have an OB and an ND, the naturopathic doctor.
They have the co-founder, who is a lawyer, actually, and then a fourth reviewer.
And, like, every article has to be reviewed by all of those because they're making specific medical claims.
And, like, it has to go out to the public, it has legal implications, it has implications to their audience, of course, for authority, all of that stuff.
The older pipeline struggled a lot, as you can imagine.
The agentic pipeline...
It has gotten us to where we can edit it.
It takes about five minutes to edit it.
They leave us about three or four comments per article, just the doctor going, oh, we should say may, you know, instead of will cure XYZ here.
And it's had some great success there.
Basically took them from a place where they were like, oh, man, we like that you're doing it, but it sucks that we have to review this so long.
It takes us so long to review each article.
And so we've managed to reduce that significantly for them.

So I think an effort there might pay off really well for you in terms of quality, but also in internalizing all of the feedback you've given us so far into properly architected context for that pipeline to use.
And now it can actually suck it in and use it all, as opposed to previously where it was sort of like able to pick a handful of things per section to use at any given time.
Okay.
Okay.
right.
We're looking forward to using that for sure.
And
Yeah, so that's it.
Yeah, I would love to take a pass at it and internalize all of the context that you've done.
I'll do some work to context engineer based on all of the feedback that you've given.
I know we did a product deep dive with you, did a couple of hours of kickoff calls.
I'll take a look at all of those and all of the feedback that you've given in the articles.
We'll scrape all of it, and I'll take a look through it and do some engineering.
I'd love to generate a couple pieces of content.
And what I would, the test that I find the most, like, useful is to find a couple of pieces that you've already edited and approved.
That you're like, hey, we like these, these are good, let's go ahead and publish these.
I'll take those and rerun them, not to make you feel bad about the old content, but that you already know what your standards are good for, are for that piece.
And we'll produce a couple, and you can take a look at the results.
This should tell us two things.
One, if they did take pipeline is sort of able to deliver a better quality article overall for you.
But also whether it is properly taking in all of the context that you've given so far.
So you can feel a little bit more at ease that you're not going to have to re-engage that entire contextual labor all over again.
Yeah.
Sounds good.
Yeah.
And then just closing the loop, I'll work with Mariana and Mara.
I've scheduled time with them in Sydney tomorrow so we can align on kind of the cadence of how we're moving forward with those pillars and the different verticals that you've talked about.
So we'll keep going in the campaign that you started just from a sort of management standpoint.
So I think hopefully with those two things, we'll see some quick improvements, but we'll keep you posted.
Looks good.
Hopefully you'll see.
Yeah, you'll feel more comfortable soon.
All right.
Cool.
Thank you.
Thank you, folks.
Thanks.
Bye.
Appreciate it.
Ciao.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Gather feedback on the recent team transition and content quality.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=37.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [The unexpected team switch from Sydney to Mariana & Mara caused a significant setback, forcing Ambient.AI to re-teach core business context and strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=449.0)
  - [Current content is disjointed and lacks the authentic, technical voice required for Ambient.AI's B2B security operator audience.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=377.0)
  - [GrowthX will deploy new "agentic pipelines" to fix this, using a larger context window (2k vs. 800-1k tokens) to generate holistic articles and reduce editing time from 30-60 to \~5 minutes.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1540.0)
  - [GrowthX will also align the new team with Ambient.AI's campaign-based strategy (e.g., "gun detection for schools") to provide a clear, thematic narrative for internal stakeholders.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=758.0)

## Topics

### The Problem: Disrupted Workflow & Content Quality

  - [The unexpected team transition from Sydney to Mariana & Mara created a major disruption, forcing Ambient.AI to repeat the time-intensive process of teaching core business context.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=449.0)
  - [**Critical Context Lost:**](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=180.0)
      - [**Audience:** B2B physical security operators with deep hands-on experience.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=201.0)
      - [**Product Nuances:** Differentiating complex AI tech in a noisy market.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=222.0)
      - [**Specific Claims:** The "95% alarm reduction" claim is use-case specific (e.g., false positives), not a general statement.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=614.0)
  - [**Content Quality Issues:**](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1445.0)
      - [Disjointed articles with tangential sections, reflecting the old pipeline's section-by-section generation method.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1445.0)
      - [Lacks an authentic, human-like voice, sounding like "marketing machine" output.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=377.0)
  - [**Strategic Misalignment:**](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=720.0)
      - [The new team's keyword-focused approach conflicts with Ambient.AI's campaign-based strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=720.0)
      - [**Ambient.AI's Goal:** Organize content around macro themes (e.g., "gun detection for schools") to create a clear narrative for internal teams like sales.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=758.0)

### The Solution: New Pipelines & Strategic Alignment

  - [GrowthX will deploy new "agentic pipelines" to address the core technical issues.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1070.0)
  - [**Pipeline Improvements:**](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1540.0)
      - [**Larger Context Window:** 2,000 tokens per generation (vs. 800-1,000) enables the AI to generate entire articles holistically, solving the "disjointed sections" problem.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1540.0)
      - [**Reduced Editing Time:** Aims to cut human editing time from 30-60 minutes to \~5 minutes per article.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1272.0)
      - [**Institutionalized Feedback:** All past feedback will be context-engineered into the pipeline's rules, ensuring consistency and preventing knowledge loss during future team changes.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1241.0)
  - [**Team Alignment:**](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1814.0)
      - [Andi will work with Mariana, Mara, and Sydney to align on Ambient.AI's campaign-based content strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1814.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Matthew:**](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1744.0)
      - [Context-engineer all past feedback and deep-dive notes into the new agentic pipelines.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1744.0)
      - [Generate test articles by re-running previously approved content through the new pipeline to validate quality and context retention.](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1766.0)
  - [**Andi:**](https://fathom.video/share/xn2NrL_t9AjsCZwGJPyQMi_uda1uCUz_?tab=summary&timestamp=1814.0)
